items.title: Write the base case first
items.avoid: Diving straight into the recursive call and adding the base case last.
items.prefer: Open every recursive function with the base case(s).
items.why: Forces you to think about termination and makes the function obviously correct on small inputs.
items.example:
```python
def length(xs):
    if not xs: return 0           # base
    return 1 + length(xs[1:])     # recursive
```

items.title: Memoize when sub-problems overlap
items.avoid: Naïve recursion that recomputes the same arguments.
items.why: Converts O(2ⁿ)/O(n!) trees into O(n) DAGs by reusing answers.
items.example:
```python
from functools import lru_cache
@lru_cache(None)
def cost(i, j): ...
```

items.title: Convert to iteration when depth is unbounded
items.why: Python's recursion limit (~1000) is generous for algorithmic depth, hostile for data depth.
items.example:
```python
stack = [root]
while stack:
    n = stack.pop()
    ...
    stack.extend(n.children)
```

items.title: Use `yield from` for recursive generators
items.avoid: `for x in walk(child): yield x` boilerplate.
items.example:
```python
def walk(n):
    if n is None: return
    yield n.value
    for c in n.children:
        yield from walk(c)
```

items.title: Avoid mutable default arguments
items.why: Default args are shared across calls; state leaks between independent invocations.
